Considering that with this version upgrade of hypercorn we would basically not be able to forward the headers as they are received, I think we should maybe try to start a discussion in the hypercorn repo if there's going to be any way of retrieving the raw / original headers again.

Even though the HTTP spec clearly states that headers are case insensitive, I think lots of systems where both -  the client and the server - are developed by the same entity might suffer from this change. Also, I think it might not even be fully intentional, since the root cause of the change might be an somewhat older commit (https://github.com/pgjones/hypercorn/commit/058e84f5cb418fca40cabc991d40c87dfc44a39e) where the commit message indicates that it has been changed to satisfy new black / mypy checks.

Thank you very much for noticing and proposing a fix for this! But I think there's a much simpler fix that will achieve the same goal.

I think doing the following would prevent adding new complexity to `.ci/test.sh`:

1. replace `treelearner: ` in `.github/workflows/cuda.yml` with `task:`
2. remove this line: https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/702db13b01bfb090c3f8f3455790a498f105b28f/.github/workflows/cuda.yml#L14
3. change the following line to `TASK="${{ matrix.task }}"`: https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/702db13b01bfb090c3f8f3455790a498f105b28f/.github/workflows/cuda.yml#L89

I remember we had a discussion in #4630 about not adding more complexity to `test.sh`: https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/4630#discussion_r820284099.

-----

In addition to that request...right now, both `cuda_exp` CI jobs are failing with the following error

> Fatal Python error: Aborted

([build link](https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/runs/7991788198?check_suite_focus=true))

We should never merge a PR that will result in CI being broken on `master`, so please do one of these:

* push a fix for that issue to this PR
* remove the `cuda_exp` CI jobs in this PR, document the work to add them back in a separate issue, merge this PR, and then push a follow-up PR fixing `cuda_exp` and adding back the CI jobs

I hope a fix can just be pushed here, but I don't know how complicated it will be to debug this. If it is very complicated, then we might as well eliminate those two `cuda_exp` jobs that are not actually testing `cuda_exp`, to save CI time.

And if the "make an issue and fix this later" approach is taken, then I think we should not merge *any* more `cuda_exp` PRs until those CI jobs have been re-enabled.

@pacospace  PTAL

Hello,

Thanks for all the work and the major modifications in your code, it is now in my opinion in a much better shape and we are approaching a merge. 

Still I have a few important modifications now that I better understand what your proposed solvers actually do.  I know it can get tiresome but POT is becoming very large and we need to be very careful with positioning new API and solvers.

My understanding is that they are MMOT solvers for marginal distribution that have a support on a regular 1D grid and using a specific ground metric (max-min that has monge property) that corresponds to the absolute value loss/EMD ground metric with two marginals.

This is very interesting and I want it in POT but it also means that we cannot use the very general names of the method that you chose in your published paper because it would lead to much confusion for POT users. So I proposed more precise names below that describe I think better what is solved and done. I provide more specific comments below (for instance having mathematical object follow the name of function parameters).

On a more scientific question I am surprised by the shape or your "barycenter" that is actually from my understanding a convergence point for the minimization of your MMOT formulation. It seems to be very similar to L2 (average) barycenters especially compared to the LP solution. Do you have an intuition why?

- Refactor the agent manager and make it manage the dashboard agent.
- Move runtime env agent to the new place and make the old dashboard agent use that. In the mean time, make the runtime env agent able to start.

- Integrate it with raylet to replace the old ones and don't start runtime env agent in the old one by default.

LGTM. I want to point out that, in IEEE 802.15.4 spec, it points out the RF power measurement should be done at antenna port.

> 10.1.4 RF power measurement
Unless otherwise stated, all RF power measurements, either transmit or receive, shall be made at the appropriate transceiver to antenna connector. The measurements shall be made with equipment that is either matched to the impedance of the antenna connector or corrected for any mismatch. For devices without an antenna connector, the measurements shall be interpreted as effective isotropic radiated power (EIRP) and any radiated measurements shall be corrected to compensate for the antenna gain in the implementation.

So when using cli to set/get CCA threshold, I think user would expect to set/get the real CCA measurement on antenna port and will not need to take into consideration of any external FEM used or board attenuation. I.E. for two boards with and without external FEM, setting CCA threshold of -74dBm should have similar effects on both boards. This FEM related gain/attenuation should be handled in `Radio` layer and reflected in `otPlatRadioSetCcaEnergyDetectThreshold`.

Well done on creating a pull request! Roughly the same feature was proposed before by @kunif in #91. Unfortunately, his work is unfinished, but there are some things he did right: - He used `-p` as a short option instead of `-t`. `-p` has the advantage that it doesn't conflict with existing options of `pip list` and `pip install`. - He dynamically sized the column widths, so the data always fit comfortably in the table. - He ensured that the way the available updates are displayed (raw, wordy or tabular) was (mostly) independent of the way they are installed (automatically, interactively or not at all). This is not the case in the current version of your proposal: you cannot have a table and automatic installation at the same time. - He documented the new feature in the README. I suggest you read and learn from #91, including the comments, and also reuse the work that was already done over there, including the example code that I wrote in the comments. One particular way in which you might approach this, is as follows: 1. Add @kunif's fork as a remote to your local clone.
`git remote add -f kunif https://github.com/kunif/pip-review.git` 2. Create a new branch, based on @kunif's branch. This enables you to build on top of their hard work. Also, when your pull request is accepted, @kunif will automatically be credited as well.
`git checkout -b table-option kunif/add-preview-options` 3. Make the changes I suggested in #91. You are welcome to just blatantly copy the code I wrote. Commit these changes before continuing. 4. Make any other changes you want to make. Maybe you want to change `--preview` into `--table`. Maybe you can find a way so that the extra `-P` option is not needed for people who want a table and no installation (arguably, this is what should happen by default when only the `-p` option is passed, which is also how your current proposal behaves). Commit at least once along the way. 5. Push your `table-option` branch to your fork. 6. Update the current pull request to request that your `table-option` branch is merged instead of your `develop` branch. Failing that (if the option is not available, or it does not work for whatever reason), submit a new pull request instead. Some more comments below. Good luck and please let me know if you have any questions!

looks great overall! just a couple of questions/nits

one broader questions i want to clarify is what assumptions we are making when accessing the payload of the request object.

if the request is a form request and contains a form-encoded body, the following calls will return
```python
request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads from the stream, caches the body, and returns it
request.form                   # parses the form data from the cache and returns the form data
request.get_data(as_text=True) # returns the cached body (will contain the original form-encoded body!)
````

whereas calling:
```
request.data                   # reads from the stream, parses the form data, caches the form data and caches the remaining body (which is now empty, and will return `""`)

request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads and returns the cached body (which is empty)
request.form                   # returns the cached form data
request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads and returns the cached body (which is empty)
```

see here for a detailed explanation: https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/blob/bb21bf90b0b121e3ed45b9950b823e4b43a81fd8/src/werkzeug/wrappers/request.py#L385-L390

so that the parser will potentially behave differently depending on previous calls to `request.data` or `request.get_data(parse_form_data=True)`.
i'm not sure exactly how to address this.

after writing all this and thinking a bit more about the werkzeug API, i think it may be the best call to NOT call `get_data` at all, and just rely on the properties `request.data`, `request.values`, `request.form`, `request.args`, etc, which seems to be the way it was intended.
you lose the bit of convenience that `as_text=True` provides, but at then the behavior will be consistent.

I am sorry this cost you so much time, and this is super confusing. But the default actually is correct here.
The issue is that the `timestream-query` operations are better maintained in the specs than `timestream-write` operations. The `ListTagsForResources` is a perfect example:
- `timestream-write` just defines the mandatory parameters.
- `timestream-query` also defines the additional optional parameters.

This is why the default should be `timestream-query`, because these operations are more flexible and better maintained (at least this is our subjective conclusion).
You can easily implement the `ListTagsForResource` by implementing it in the `TimestreamQueryApi` for both APIs:
```python
class TimestreamQueryProvider(TimestreamQueryApi):
    ...
    def list_tags_for_resource(
        self,
        context: RequestContext,
        resource_arn: AmazonResourceName,
        max_results: MaxTagsForResourceResult = None,
        next_token: NextTagsForResourceResultsToken = None,
    ) -> ListTagsForResourceResponse:
        # This function is valid for both, timestream-query and timestream-write,
        # since max_results and next_token are optional.
        # Both providers operate on the same resources / databases.
        ...
        return ListTagsForResourceResponse(...)
```
This should work perfectly fine (the routing worked correctly in my quick test).

Yep, this looks about right. A few comments:

1. It's probably better to use serialized JSON instead of checking individual fields, b/c it catches more changes as we make them. It's saved us a lot of times while tweaking parsers. Have you found the examples of this in the test suite?

2. This will need to be added to `__init__.py` to make it accessible.

3. In terms of JSON schema, I suppose this should try to match the docket schema as closely as possible. (You can find a gazillion examples of this in the docket parser tests.) By making it match, it *should* make our ingestion pipeline able to use existing code.

4. There are a handful of things we do to normalize content in the docket parser. Things like tweaking the case name to make it a bit normalized, cleaning out bad data, etc. For example, `lib.string_utils.harmonize` is usually applied to the case name, `lib.string_utils.clean_string` is a good one to clean up messy strings, and `lib.string_utils.convert_date_string` is a nice way to get dates from strings with a bit of robustness.

I think that's it for now. Looks basically right though, yes.

I have not studied the code, but it seems to work.  I tried it out on `plone.app.event`.  With `z3c.dependencychecker` master it says no problem (well, `GenericSetup` missing, but let's ignore that here).  With the branch from this PR it complains about all kinds of missing dependencies, like `Acquisition` (part of `Zope` in our mapping) and `zope.annotation`.  When I add `Zope` to the requirements, it stops complaining.

I think we should add `Plone` to the umbrella distributions for the same reason.  The [`Plone`](https://github.com/plone/Plone/) package has no code of its own, it only pulls in dependencies. The implementation here looks good. The main question is whether or not we want to do this. 

The main benefit is that a somewhat large dependency becomes optional. The primary downside is that there are a small subset of functions that will not be usable if the user has not installed matplotlib. As far as I can tell, these are:

`skimage.draw.polygon_clip`
`skimage.draw.polygon_perimeter`
`skimage.draw.rectangle_perimeter`
`skimage.filters.try_all_threshold`
`skimage.future.graph.show_rag`
`skimage.future.manual_lasso_segmentation`
`skimage.future.manual_polygon_segmentation`

I think I am weakly in favor of having this as an optional dependency, since there are likely many users that do not need the above functions. I would guess that a majority of these will already have Matplotlib or not care if it gets pulled in to a scikit-image install. For any users who really want a minimal install of scikit-image, though, this would help them.

> having a module level singleton dispatcher object that you use to subscribe and unsubscribe?

An interesting idea. That would create a module-level pubsub letting anything inside graph/ listen to events such as TEMPLATES_UPDATED. For now, I prefer the class-level Dispatcher, mainly because it is more tightly scoped. There are only a handful of classes that need to be able to listen to Hierarchy events so far, and they all already own a Hierarchy.

I realized I made a mistake in my last patch, where the tf-graph-scene adds a listener, when it doesn't own a Hierarchy. Other classes, such as tf-graph-minimap, tf-graph-controls, are similar.

If there was a use case for other modules/features outside of graph/ to listen for changes in, say, selectedNode, then I think having a singleton dispatcher at the graph/ level makes more sense.

@ArturNiederfahrenhorst I just did a more thorough pass on your LSTM tests. The test is not really testing the rollout of LSTM cells during training (test_forward_train)

What you are doing right now:
- stacking obs, actions, and states in multiple BATCHES. Where is the time dimension then?
- This is like using LSTM cells as a fully connected cell :) 

What you should be doing:
- stack obs, and actions in both batches and times. So that the final shape of batch is (bxt, ho) and the final shape of actions is (bxt, ha)
- stack the state_in to be of shape (b, n_layer=1, hs) 
- if the number of steps are not compatible with (b, t) reshaping you need to zero pad. For example if you took 30 steps and your max_rollout_len = 20. You need to create a (1, 20, ho) obs tensor and concat if with a (1, 10, ho) obs. You cannot. So you need to zero pad the second one to make it (1, 20, ho) and take care of zeros with masking. the obs shape in this case should be come (2x20, ho) with bunch of zeros in there. In this example your state_in shape should be (2, 1, hs). 

Makes sense? I guess what you are testing assumes every sequence is of length 1 (max_rollout_length=1). That's why you don't see any errors.
All I am saying is that the lstm part of this test does not have converage over an important aspect of LSTM models which is the rollout across time. I would recommend taking out the lstm stuff in forward_train from your test. your rollout test seems correct. 

Do the lstm forward_train in a separate method and PR. I can do that too and you can keep your focus on catalog stuff?

LGTM! It seems nothing needs to be released for Django 3.1 support to happen -- but this ensures that any coming changes are compatible. And thanks for spelling it out in the `setup.py` as well :+1:

Maybe using a [`Timer`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/threading.html#timer-objects) would make things cleaner.

- Every handler execution starts a new timer (unless we don't have enough "buffer"/threshold).
- Whenever the original handler finishes running (the new `finally` block in this PR) and a timer has been started, call `timer.cancel()` -- no additional condition.
- The Timer runs a function that raises the timeout exception, no conditions.

The above is my suggestion.

---

**Side note**
Because the runtime can abort/pause the handler at any time, I imagine there'd be room for the timer firing incorrectly into a future handler execution, but not sure if there's anything we could do about it. (I thought of an Event to mark the end of the handler and the timer could check it, but that doesn't change anything if the `finally` block of one execution runs into the next execution -- depends how the timeout is implemented by the runtime.)

Hi @mattwwarren! Thank you for this. I've gone through and noted a few issues that would need to be addressed before landing, but the central issue is handling of the `application/form-data`.

Looking at #289 again I think that I may have misinterpreted what you were after. Did you want to _set the Content-Type header of an application/x-www-form-urlencoded request to application/form-data_? That doesn't make sense, as the server will fail to parse it due to the content-type mismatch, won't it? Or did you want to generate an application/form-data request without actual files? The latter is actually supported — you can do `treq.post(..., files=[('field', 'value'), ...])` (or even pass a `dict` to *files*). You could also accomplish this by passing a `MultiPartProducer()` instance as the *data* parameter, along with the matching Content-Type header.

I'm left wondering if this is actually a documentation issue (of which, admittedly, there are many). The ability to override the Content-Type of a JSON request still seems useful to me, but it's a smaller feature than what you've implemented here.

Again, thank you for the PR! I'd love to work with you to make treq work for your use case.

First off, thank you for this work. There are some considerable gems in here and I am appreciating the conversation this initiates. As for my review, I am finding it difficult to focus on the large number of actual proposed changes in light of the equally large number of formatting changes. To help me get traction, I will focus on commenting on the high level changes I see proposed here:

1. Moving managing Order over to the OrdersClient does make sense from a separation of concerns point of view.
2. I would further advocate for managing (paged)Orders to the OrdersClient as well, and possibly spending some time on design of a `pager` that, similar to the `waiter`, can be abstracted across all clients (as aws does)
3. I am curious why you are advocating for having `models` manage the Order dataclass. Could OrdersClient do the same just as well? While it does seem nice to have one 'go to' for a data structure (aka `models` here), it seems to tightly couple OrdersClient to models, as evidenced by the `orders` module importing from `models` and the `models` tests import from the `orders` module.
4. Regarding the proposal to use JSON as IO in the CLI and data structures as IO for the Python library, I think this is an excellent discussion point and may be evidence that we could be well served by going through the Python library public interface, similar to what we've done for the CLI, and coming to a consensus.

> I also needed a different interface to the Credentials API, since I wanted to control the encoding when calling `CredWrite` and also add the JSON attribute. So I added a `cffi`-based interface directly to `Windows.py`. This is ugly, but I'm not adept at setuptools and wasn't sure how to properly add a new file to the system. I also don't know if I updated `setup.cfg` correcly to reflect the new dependency on `cffi`. I think it is more maintainable to have the code under `keyring`'s roof, rather than relying on the Enthought package.

I'd like to revisit this consideration. While I do like the idea of not being dependent on another implementation, it means keyring is essentially creating its own re-implementation of the Enthought package and adopting the burden that goes along with that (separate maintenance burden, diverging implementations, loss of re-use).

If we step back, I'd like to capture an issue for why pywin32-ctypes is inadequate (probably upstream in that project) and consider solving that issue first before chosing to fork a new implementation. I do think it's plausible that the upstream implementation isn't flexible enough for keyring's needs due to its legacy association with pywin32 and the interfaces it provided. I would like to shed _that_ legacy.

If we decide to adopt a unique implementation, I'd like to consider whether cffi is the best approach. Adding a dependency on cffi adds a _binary_ dependency for keyring, meaning anyone needing keyring cannot install from source without having a build environment set up. Historically, that was a much bigger problem before the advent of eggs/wheels and their common adoption in PyPI. This issue was recently [reported in twine](https://github.com/pypa/twine/issues/837#issuecomment-979275593) when users of Alpine Linux were required to install keyring and by extension cffi, but could not.

Finally, while I believe the sharding implementation here is sound enough (and provably so with the proposed additional tests), I'd like to consider a refactoring that would separate the concerns (sharded vs. unsharded). In particular, I imagine a `SimpleKeyring` that implements `WinVaultKeyring` as currrently implemented and then a specialized subclass `ShardedKeyring` that implements the sharding behavior. Even if the `SimpleKeyring` is disabled (priority -1), it would provide a means to easily test without the sharding behavior and also make it clearer what functionality is pertinent to that concern. That separation of concerns is apparent in this diff, but it's going to be much harder to reason about going forward.

To summarize, here's how I'd like to move forward:

1. Determine if pywin32-ctypes can support the necessary interfaces herein and at least capture any shortcomings.
2. Port the existing backend to an API that supports the necessary interfaces (perhaps an updated version of pywin32-ctypes or maybe a bespoke implementation). Cut a release to get early feedback on any regressions related to that change.
3. Implement the ShardedKeyring as a specialized subclass or mix-in, encapsulating the sharding logic in one class.

This incremental approach, while it may take longer, should give us better visibility into the effects of different changes.

Thanks very much for this proposal and for the list of examples!

I did some research tonight and agree with your proposal... even though `stats::predict()` is not opinionated about the name of the keyword argument referring to "data to create predictions from" in S3 methods that extend it, `newdata` is the most popular name used.

In addition to the packages mentioned in this PR's description, I also looked at a few other packages in the CRAN Machine Learning Task View ([link](https://cran.r-project.org/web/views/MachineLearning.html)).

| package | signature of `predict()` S3 method | code link   |
|:--------:|-----------------------------------:|:-----:|
| `{arules}` | `predict.itemMatrix(object, newdata, ...)` | [link](https://github.com/mhahsler/arules/blob/dfe0a7b7a0d68be5aa4c4ee4a1cac2c574d3270b/R/predict.R#L66-L67) |
| `{caret}` | `predict.train(object, newdata, ...)`        | [link](https://github.com/topepo/caret/blob/be921481bcdd5492beee4f8a59ae57e69a2c8047/pkg/caret/R/predict.train.R#L109) |
| `{catboost}` | `catboost.caret$predict(modelFit, newdata, ...)` | [link](https://github.com/catboost/catboost/blob/7d293bfb103d3891f1839ccb7d799a8e6d4a36ad/catboost/R-package/R/catboost.caret.R#L106) |
| `{C5.0}` | `predict.C5.0(object, newdata, ...)` | [link](https://github.com/topepo/C5.0/blob/807161d42af421ae6700e4b608d8b65edc9c8a08/R/predict.C5.0.R#L71-L73) |
| `{gbm}` | `predict.gbm(object, newdata, ...)` | [link](https://github.com/gbm-developers/gbm/blob/18e595597e19c289328f5eb0b73945a1ea72fc7d/R/predict.gbm.R#L5) |
| `{mboost}` | `predict.mboost(object, newdata, ...)` | [link](https://github.com/cran/mboost/blob/128a48aa7fc77ec6419ef05f0b29789337fc6fa0/R/methods.R#L25) |
| `{randomForest}` | `predict.randomForest(object, newdata, ...)` | [link](https://github.com/cran/randomForest/blob/6cedb5fbf26ca857afda687f42c5ba535d61eaf0/R/predict.randomForest.R#L2) |
| `{rpart}` | `predict.rpart(object, newdata, ...)` | [link](https://github.com/cran/rpart/blob/9994543c62ae212b3cd582c50985e0748f8ead1b/R/predict.rpart.R#L1) |
| `{stats}` | `predict.glm(object, newdata = NULL, ...)` | [link](https://github.com/wch/r-source/blob/79298c499218846d14500255efd622b5021c10ec/src/library/stats/R/predict.glm.R#L20) |
| `{stats}` | `predict.lm(object, newdata, ...)` | [link](https://github.com/wch/r-source/blob/79298c499218846d14500255efd622b5021c10ec/src/library/stats/R/lm.R#L667) | `{xgboost}` | `predict.xgb.Booster(object, newdata, ...)` | [link](https://github.com/dmlc/xgboost/blob/18a4af63aa2f99f5941fe36cb147829d68aa42d1/R-package/R/xgb.Booster.R#L327) |

Examples of other patterns:

| package | signature of `predict()` S3 method | code link   |

| `{lightgbm}` | `predict.lgb.Booster(object, data, ...)` | [link](https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/5631366175f68a28bd174982cca16975f7fb48af/R-package/R/lgb.Booster.R#L781-L782) |

<hr>

Given that...I support changing this argument name in `predict.lgb.Booster()`, as you've proposed. And I'm ok with this being a breaking change from the perspective of anyone using keyword arguments instead of positional arguments. Since `newdata` doesn't have a default argument, any existing code like (`predict(bst, data = X)`) will break with a loud error like `argument "newdata" is missing, with no default`.

Normally I'd say this kind of breaking change should go through a round of deprecation warnings, but support making it now for the same reasons mentioned in https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/5007#issue-1138103040.

@StrikerRUS or @Laurae2 at your earliest convenience, could you give your opinion on whether or not we should merge this breaking change as-in?

```python-console
 <Version('1.3.0')>: '04t4R0000010XxDQAQ', <Version('1.4.0')>: '04t4R0000010XxfQAI', <Version('1.5.0')>: '04t4R0000010XxWQAQ',
 <Version('1.6.0')>: '04t4R0000010XxUQAQ', <Version('1.7.0')>: '04t4R0000010XxwQAA', <Version('1.8.0')>: '04t4R0000010XxEQAQ',
 <Version('1.9.0')>: '04t4R000001VXxvQAO', <Version('1.10.0')>: '04t4R000001VXxgQAG', <Version('2.5.0')>: '04t4R0000010ZzEQAQ'}
```
Which means `get_newer_released_version_objs` can become:
```python-console
>>> {k:v for k,v in all_versions.items() if k > version.parse('1.5.0')}
{<Version('1.6.0')>: '04t4R0000010XxUQAQ', <Version('1.7.0')>: '04t4R0000010XxwQAA', <Version('1.8.0')>: '04t4R0000010XxEQAQ',
 <Version('1.9.0')>: '04t4R000001VXxvQAO', <Version('1.10.0')>: '04t4R000001VXxgQAG', <Version('2.5.0')>: '04t4R0000010ZzEQAQ'}
```

And `get_older_released_version_objs` becomes:
```python-console
>>> {k:v for k,v in all_versions.items() if k < version.parse('1.5.0')}
{<Version('1.0.0')>: '04t4R0000019XxSQAU', <Version('1.1.0')>: '04t4R0000019XxIQAU', <Version('1.2.0')>: '04t4R0000010XxrQAA',
 <Version('1.3.0')>: '04t4R0000010XxDQAQ', <Version('1.4.0')>: '04t4R0000010XxfQAI'}
```

This also works for `PatchVersions` without any special handling:
```python-console
>>> all_versions[version.parse('1.5.1')] = '04t4R0000010ZyEQAQ'
>>> {k:v for k,v in all_versions.items() if k > version.parse('1.5.0')}
{<Version('1.6.0')>: '04t4R0000010XxUQAQ', <Version('1.7.0')>: '04t4R0000010XxwQAA', <Version('1.8.0')>: '04t4R0000010XxEQAQ',
 <Version('1.9.0')>: '04t4R000001VXxvQAO', <Version('1.10.0')>: '04t4R000001VXxgQAG', <Version('2.5.0')>: '04t4R0000010ZzEQAQ',
 <Version('1.5.1')>: '04t4R0000010ZyEQAQ'}
```

This'd allow us the flexibility to easily exclude specific versions:
```python-console
>>> version.Version('1.1.3') not in [version.Version('1.0.0'), version.Version('1.1.0')]
True
>>> version.Version('1.1.0') not in [version.Version('1.0.0'), version.Version('1.1.0')]
False
```

All without concatenating SOQL, etc.
